participants.reasoning: The extracted description matches the official participant summary: 55 community volunteers diagnosed with knee OA by a healthcare provider were recruited.
participants.extracted: Community-dwelling older adults aged 60 years or older with knee osteoarthritis and subtle-to-moderate cognitive impairment were recruited from 8 study sites (6 retirement apartments and 2 senior centers) between January 2008 and February 2010. Of 123 elders screened, 55 were eligible and enrolled; the 8 sites were cluster-randomized to tai chi (n=28) or attention-control education (n=27). Eligibility required an MMSE score of 18-28, self-reported or clinician-confirmed knee OA with pain, ability to speak English, physician/nurse practitioner permission, no regular exercise program in the past month, ability to walk 50 meters without assistance or a walking device, and ability to stand unsupported for 1 minute. Participants were excluded for significant sensory deficits, Parkinson’s disease, cancer pain, other major chronic pain conditions, recent arthroscopic or joint replacement surgery, recent fractures, major psychiatric disorder or untreated depressive symptoms, recent falls, or vertigo.
participants.official:
Knee OA recruitment
55 community volunteers
Diagnosed by healthcare provider

interventions.reasoning: The extracted description matches the official interventions in both the Tai Chi dose and format, and the attention-control education program is described as an in-person comparable session-based control.
interventions.extracted: The intervention was a Sun-style Tai Chi program adapted to reduce physical and cognitive demands for older adults with knee osteoarthritis and mild-to-moderate cognitive impairment. Participants in the Tai Chi arm attended three instructor-led sessions per week for 20 weeks, beginning with 20 minutes per session and gradually increasing to 40 minutes per session. The comparison group received an attention-control education program with instructor-led educational activities at the same site and for the same session length as the Tai Chi group.
interventions.official:
Clinic, supervised group setting, delivered entirely in‐person
1. Attention control; health education, 60 synchronous contact occasions
2. Exercise (Tai Chi): 60 sessions (3 x 20 to 40 minutes per week over 20 weeks); 60 synchronous contact occasions

outcomes.reasoning: The extracted outcomes include additional pain measures and secondary outcomes across repeated time points, whereas the official outcomes list only WOMAC pain and function at 20 weeks and explicitly states no quality of life or treatment success measures.
outcomes.extracted: The trial planned to measure pain and related secondary pain outcomes in community-dwelling older adults with knee osteoarthritis and cognitive impairment. The primary outcome was pain, assessed with the Western Ontario and McMaster Universities Osteoarthritis Index (WOMAC) pain subscale (reported previously for the parent trial) and, in this secondary analysis, the Verbal Descriptor Scale (VDS) for pain. The VDS is a 1-item verbal pain intensity scale scored from 0 to 6, with assessments collected at six time points: Week 1 (baseline), and Weeks 5, 9, 13, 17, and 21. Secondary pain outcomes included observed pain behaviors, measured by Keefe’s observational method using videotaped 10-minute behavior samples coded for five pain-related behaviors on a 0–100 total score scale, collected at the same six time points (Weeks 1, 5, 9, 13, 17, and 21); and analgesic intake, measured as daily acetaminophen-equivalent dosage (mg/day) derived from pill counts and medication use records, also collected at the same repeated time points across the intervention period (baseline through Week 21, with monthly follow-up measurements).
outcomes.official:
At 20 weeks (immediately post‐intervention)
Pain (WOMAC pain subscale)
Physical function (WOMAC function subscale)
No quality of life measure
No participant‐reported treatment success measure
